Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: I need to write a DuckDB SQL query that filters out cases with more than six events and returns the events from the remaining cases The table is called event_log with columns case_id activity and timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: First I need to figure out how to count the number of events per case_id So a GROUP BY case_id to get the counts Then I need to filter those groups where the count is <=6 Once I have those case_ids I need to select all the events from the original table where the case_id is in that filtered list

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Maybe using a subquery Like first select the case_ids that have count <=6 then join that back to the original table Alternatively using a CTE Common Table Expression to first get the case_ids that meet the condition

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: The query structure would be something like SELECT all from event_log WHERE case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT star <= 6

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Yes that makes sense The subquery groups by case_id applies HAVING to filter groups with count <=6 then the outer query selects all events with those case_ids

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: Alternatively using a JOIN SELECT e all FROM event_log e JOIN SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT star <=6 AS valid_cases ON e case_id equals valid_cases case_id Either approach should work

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The first one is simpler Let me check for possible issues For example if there are multiple case_ids with the same count but that is okay The subquery correctly groups and filters

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The HAVING clause is necessary here because we need to filter after aggregation Using WHERE COUNT would not work because WHERE is before grouping
